Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard metadata and a VCS `source` using `git+https://github.com/sacredbanana/lab3d-sdl`, which is consistent with the project's stated upstream. The `sha256sums` array uses 'SKIP', which is expected and required for VCS sources in AUR packages—this is not a security issue at this stage.

The top-level variables and `pkgver()` function are evaluated when sourcing the PKGBUILD. The `pkgver()` function changes into the `$_namey` directory (which resolves to `lab3d-sdl`) and runs `git rev-list` and `git rev-parse`, which are safe operations assuming the source has been cloned as intended. There is no evidence of command substitution, obfuscation, network exfiltration, or execution of untrusted code during the sourcing phase. The `pkgver()` logic is standard for git-based packages.

The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only fetch the git repository and evaluate the top-level variables and `pkgver()`, which are all benign in this context. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` functions are executed under these flags, so deeper issues (if any) are out of scope for this narrow gate.
</details>
<summary>
Sourcing PKGBUILD and fetching sources is safe
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for a Git-based package that builds <code>lab3d-sdl</code> from its upstream source at https://github.com/sacredbanana/lab3d-sdl, which matches the declared URL. The <code>source</code> array uses <code>git+https</code>, a standard practice for VCS packages. The <code>sha256sums=('SKIP')</code> is appropriate because Git sources are inherently mutable, and checksums cannot be pinned.

The <code>pkgver()</code> function updates the version based on the Git history, a common and safe practice. The <code>build()</code> and <code>package()</code> functions use standard <code>cmake</code> commands to compile and install the package into <code>$pkgdir</code>, with no evidence of obfuscated or suspicious commands. Dependencies are reasonable for an SDL-based game (e.g., <code>sdl2</code>, <code>glu</code>) and no unauthorized network access or file operations are performed.

There are no signs of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, unauthorized downloads, or execution of untrusted code. All operations align with standard AUR packaging practices for a VCS-based open-source game port.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR VCS package with safe build process
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for version.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `version.cmake` contains only three `set()` commands that define version variables (LAB3D_VERSION_MAJOR, LAB3D_VERSION_MINOR, LAB3D_VERSION_PATCH) with numeric values. These are standard CMake directives used to specify versioning information for the project and do not perform any system operations, file modifications, network requests, or code execution. There is no use of dangerous commands such as `exec`, `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or `base64`, nor any obfuscation or suspicious patterns.

This file is consistent with typical version configuration files in CMake-based projects. It does not introduce any executable logic or side effects. The content is static and declarative, posing no security risk. No data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized access mechanisms are present or implied.

The absence of any executable or I/O operations, combined with the clear and standard use of CMake syntax for versioning, confirms this file is safe.
</details>
<summary>
Standard CMake version file, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for clean_builds.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `clean_builds.sh` is a standard build cleanup utility commonly found in AUR packages, particularly those involving CMake or other build systems. It defines lists of directories and files typically generated during a build process (e.g., `CMakeCache.txt`, `CMakeFiles`, `Makefile`) and uses `find` to recursively locate and remove them from a designated build directory. All operations are confined to the local `build` directory and only target files and directories expected from prior builds.

The script does not perform any suspicious actions such as downloading external content, executing untrusted code, accessing sensitive system files, or exfiltrating data. Commands like `rm -rf` and `rm -f` are used appropriately in the context of cleaning build artifacts. The use of `echo` before deletion provides a trace of actions, improving transparency. There is no obfuscation, unexpected network activity, or privilege escalation.

This script aligns with common developer workflows and poses no inherent security risk. While it modifies the filesystem, it does so in a predictable and limited scope for legitimate build maintenance purposes.
</details>
<summary>
Build cleanup script, no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

The structure and content are consistent with standard CMake usage and pose no security risk. No suspicious patterns (e.g., `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, `exec`) are present, and the file appears to be purely for development environment configuration.

The variable `KEN_64BIT` is set based on architecture, which is expected behavior.

No evidence of obfuscation, malicious payloads, or unauthorized data exfiltration is present. This is a benign project configuration file serving a normal development purpose.

LLM auditresponse for build-linux.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `build-linux.sh` is a standard build automation script that uses Docker Buildx to compile multi-platform Linux binaries for a project. It creates a buildx builder instance named "kenbuilder" if it does not already exist, switches to using it, and then builds the Docker image targeting multiple architectures (amd64, i386, arm64, arm/v7, ppc64le), outputting the results to the `dist` directory. All operations are local and involve only Docker commands with no network data exfiltration, unexpected downloads, or execution of unverified remote code.

There are no suspicious operations such as `curl|bash`, `eval`, or base64-encoded payloads. The script uses standard Docker functionality in a transparent manner, consistent with reproducible cross-platform builds. While it modifies the Docker context state by creating and selecting a builder instance, these actions are limited to the local Docker environment and do not pose a security threat. The use of `set -e` ensures the script fails fast on errors, which is good practice.

The only minor concern is the lack of input validation or sandboxing if this script were executed in a shared or untrusted environment, but this is a developer tool, not a package installation script. As written, it does not introduce supply-chain risks.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Docker build script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for SDL_main.h:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `SDL_main.h` is a standard header from the SDL (Simple DirectMedia Layer) library, providing platform-specific redefinition of the `main` function to support consistent entry points across different operating systems. It contains declarations for functions like `SDL_main`, `SDL_SetMainReady`, and platform-specific initialization routines (e.g., `SDL_WinRTRunApp`, `SDL_GDKRunApp`, `SDL_UIKitRunApp`), all of which are part of SDL's legitimate cross-platform abstraction.

The content shows no signs of malicious activity. There are no network operations, file manipulations, shell commands, or obfuscated code. The header is licensed under a permissive zlib-style license and includes standard platform detection and function declarations intended to help applications integrate with platform-specific runtime requirements (such as Win32, Android, iOS, GDK). These are normal and expected behaviors for a multimedia library like SDL.

This file is part of the upstream SDL source code and is not related to packaging scripts or build-time payloads. Its functionality is confined to supporting application startup across diverse platforms, which is consistent with the library’s documented purpose. No supply-chain threats are present.
</details>
<summary>
Benign header file from SDL2 library
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for switch.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `switch.cmake` is a CMake toolchain configuration file used to set up cross-compilation for the Nintendo Switch using devkitPro. It defines compiler paths, flags, and target architecture (aarch64) in a standard and expected manner. All paths and binaries referenced are consistent with devkitPro's layout, such as `$ENV{DEVKITPRO}/devkitA64/bin` and `$ENV{DEVKITPRO}/libnx`, which are legitimate directories for the development environment. The file does not execute arbitrary commands, download external content, or perform any file operations outside of standard CMake logic.

The configuration sets up CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_* to control where dependencies are searched, which is a common and safe practice in cross-compilation setups. There is no obfuscation, suspicious network activity, or execution of unverified code. The use of environment variables like `DEVKITPRO` is normal for toolchain files and does not introduce security risks when used in this context. This file aligns with expected practices in the homebrew development community for Nintendo Switch.

Overall, the file serves a clear and legitimate purpose in the build process without introducing malicious or risky behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Standard toolchain configuration for Nintendo Switch development</summary>
</security_assessment>

[7/10] Reviewing src/lab3d-sdl/CMakeLists.txt, src/lab3d-sdl/build-switch.sh, src/lab3d-sdl/mkdist.sh...
+ Reviewed src/lab3d-sdl/switch.cmake. Status: SAFE -- Standard toolchain configuration for Nintendo Switch development
LLM auditresponse for mkdist.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script mkdist.sh is a build utility script used to package source and binary files into compressed archives (tar.gz and zip) for distribution. It defines functions to copy specific files and directories (source code, textures, binaries, data) into versioned directories and then compress them. All operations are confined to local file copying and archiving using standard commands like cp, tar, and zip. The script does not perform any network operations, execute downloaded code, or use obfuscated commands. It only handles expected project files, such as source code (.c, .h, Makefile), assets (wallparams.ini, hires, icons), and binaries (ken, ken.exe, libraries), which are typical components of a game or application release.

The only conditional user input is the DATE parameter, used to structure output paths under dist/, and the script validates its presence. There are no eval statements, base64 decoding, curl/wget calls, or other suspicious constructs. The functions copy_winbin, copy_linbin32, copy_linbin64, etc., reference local system paths under ./dist/ and do not introduce external dependencies at runtime. While the script is not part of the PKGBUILD itself (it's a maintainer tool), it operates within expected packaging workflows and shows no signs of malicious intent. Actions such as rm -f and recursive cp are used appropriately in a build context and do not target sensitive system locations.
</details>
<summary>
Packaging script with no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[8/10] Reviewing src/lab3d-sdl/CMakeLists.txt, src/lab3d-sdl/build-switch.sh...
+ Reviewed src/lab3d-sdl/mkdist.sh. Status: SAFE -- Packaging script with no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for build-switch.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `build-switch.sh` is a build automation helper that uses Docker to run a build inside the official `devkitpro/devkita64` container for Nintendo Switch development. It mounts the current directory (`${PWD}`) into the container at `/work`, sets user/group IDs for file ownership, and runs `make clean package -f Makefile.Switch` inside the container.

All operations are confined to the current project directory and standard build processes. The container image `devkitpro/devkita64` is the official DevkitPro toolchain image for Switch development, which is a trusted and well-known resource in the homebrew Switch community. No unauthorized network requests, file modifications outside the build context, or obfuscated commands are present. The use of Docker with volume mounts and environment variables for UID/GID mapping is standard practice for reproducible builds in containerized environments.

While mounting the current directory into a container carries inherent risk if the container image were malicious, the image used here is from a legitimate source, and no code outside the stated build process is fetched or executed. There is no data exfiltration, no unexpected file system access, and no dynamic command injection.
</details>
<summary>
Standard containerized build script using trusted toolchain
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing src/lab3d-sdl/CMakeLists.txt...
+ Reviewed src/lab3d-sdl/build-switch.sh. Status: SAFE -- Standard containerized build script using trusted toolchain
LLM auditresponse for CMakeLists.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The CMakeLists.txt file defines a standard build configuration for a cross-platform SDL2-based game application, "Kens-Labyrinth". It includes typical CMake patterns for setting up project structure, handling platform-specific requirements (Windows, Linux, macOS), linking libraries, and packaging. The file references bundled dependencies (SDL2, zlib, etc.) located in the `external/` directory, which is common for self-contained builds. It also configures code signing and bundle settings for macOS, and sets up CPack for generating Debian packages, which are standard practices in legitimate build systems.

No suspicious behavior was detected. There are no network calls, downloads, obfuscated commands, or attempts to exfiltrate data. All file operations are confined to the project's own directories (e.g., copying assets to the distribution folder). The use of `codesign` on macOS is part of normal application bundling and is not executed unless the user explicitly builds for that platform. Similarly, the Debian packaging setup refers only to installing the game's own files into standard system paths, with appropriate metadata and dependencies.

The build script respects user-controlled variables (e.g., code signing identity via environment) but does not hardcode or transmit sensitive information. Everything present aligns with expected build and packaging workflows for a native application targeting multiple platforms through CMake.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior detected; standard CMake build script
</summary>
</security_assessment>
